channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: Why the most expensive Seed deals are the cheapest | E2299
gist: A VC roundtable (Tomasz Tunguz/Theory Ventures, Michael Downey/Castalia Capital, Paige Doherty/Behind Genius Ventures, host Alex) argues 2026 will be a banner liquidity year led by ~$3.5T of SpaceX/OpenAI/Anthropic IPOs, that AI has reset the growth bar (10x+/yr now needed for a strong Series A) and shifted power back to founders, and that the top 1-5% of seed deals may be underpriced because exit outcomes keep getting structurally larger. Recurring threads: tokens/compute-for-equity financialization, model-routing/skill-distillation pushing inference on-prem and to open source, and verticalized 'hyper-specialized' applied AI as where value accrues.

insights.claim: Tunguz expects broad-based 2026 liquidity, noting the SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic IPOs together would raise more dollars than the sum of all IPOs in the prior decade, signaling massive investor demand for AI and space exposure.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: If correct, the IPO window reopens broadly in 2026, pulling forward exits and re-rating private late-stage marks; watch the order book for follow-on filings.
insights.quote: if they raise what they intended to, would be greater than the sum total of all IPOs, dollars raised in the previous decade

insights.claim: The SpaceX IPO is reportedly 2.5-3x oversubscribed and priced at a single flat $135/share with no range, an unusually take-it-or-leave-it offering structure for a deal of this size.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Heavy oversubscription at a fixed price implies a likely first-day pop and post-IPO volatility; pricing power sits entirely with the issuer.
insights.quote: Elon's offering one number, take it or leave it. It's overs subscribed.

insights.claim: Bending Spoons filed to IPO as an 'AI holding company' that buys legacy businesses (including AOL) and reinvigorates them with AI-native coding, a PE-rollup-style model executed under a startup banner.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: A new public-market template: buying distressed legacy software and re-platforming it with AI could become a repeatable, fundable playbook.
insights.quote: I think about them as like an AI holding company where they buy legacy businesses and then reinvigorate them with AI native coding practices

insights.claim: Multiple panelists plan to wait rather than buy the SpaceX IPO at open, expecting an initial surge followed by a pullback; Downey specifically intends to buy just after the midterms anticipating a political-driven market reset.
insights.speaker: panel (Tunguz, Downey, Doherty)
insights.so_what: Sophisticated insiders signal they'd fade the IPO pop and accumulate on post-midterm weakness rather than chase the open.
insights.quote: I'm going to wait until the midterms, just after the midterms

insights.claim: Doherty highlights that SpaceX's S-1 frames energy as the core bottleneck for AI, including using the sun to generate energy for AI workloads.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Reframes SpaceX partly as an energy-for-AI play, linking its valuation thesis to the power-constraint narrative dominating AI infrastructure.
insights.quote: one of the core points was we're going to use the sun to make energy for AI

insights.claim: All three panelists said their interest in the three mega-IPOs is highly price-sensitive, indicating willingness to buy if valuations were cut to ~$1 trillion, implying current entry valuations are viewed as rich.
insights.speaker: panel
insights.so_what: Demand is real but valuation-elastic; a meaningful drawdown would convert spectators into buyers.
insights.quote: If if I lowered the price to one trillion, would your answers change?

themes.theme: AI-Reset Growth Bar & Founder Power Shift

insights.claim: Doherty says the Series A bar has reset: 3x annual growth is now middle-of-the-pack, while AI-native companies routinely post 10x with some exceeding 100x revenue growth in a year.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Underwriting standards have stepped up; investors should re-baseline what 'fast' means and discount 3x growers at Series A.
insights.quote: it used to be like you could grow 3x and raise a great series A and now I feel like it's more you grow 10x in a year and raise a great series A

insights.claim: Doherty cites the last 100 billion-dollar-plus exits showing the average IPO now requires roughly $300-500M in annual revenue, a materially higher bar that pushes early-stage investors to ask market-size questions far earlier.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: The revenue bar to go public has risen; seed/Series A diligence must underwrite to a much larger eventual revenue base.
insights.quote: the average revenue is between 300 to 500 million in annual revenue

insights.claim: Tunguz attributes hyper-growth partly to selling into AI labs, where single contracts of tens to hundreds of millions can grow a vendor 10-50x because access to a model, dataset or technology can shift market share and tens of billions of market cap with a single release.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Lab procurement urgency creates extreme willingness-to-pay; infra/data vendors selling to labs can compound revenue abnormally fast.
insights.quote: a single contract can can grow the business 10 to 20x to 50x

insights.claim: Tunguz cites a Morgan Stanley analysis that more than 50% of corporate AI budgets are net-new, partly drawn from future labor spend, with labor spend running 3-7x larger than software spend.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Net-new budgets sourced from labor TAM mean AI software addressable spend is far larger than legacy software budgets imply.
insights.quote: More than 50% of AI budgets are net new

insights.claim: Doherty pushes back that the hottest growth is not from selling to labs but from non-lab companies skipped in prior software waves now buying application-layer AI.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Suggests durable demand sits in previously under-digitized verticals, not just the lab ecosystem, broadening the application-layer opportunity.
insights.quote: It's more like in companies that either like got skipped over in the software waves before that are now interested in buying AI applications

insights.claim: Doherty describes a portfolio company that raised only a $4M seed yet hit a ~$120M revenue run-rate generating roughly $750k/month in free cash flow, illustrating capital-efficient AI scaling that makes further fundraising optional.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: AI-native companies can reach scale on tiny capital, shifting leverage to founders and reducing dependence on VC.
insights.quote: they only raised a $4 million seed round. third $120 million revenue run rate right right now

insights.claim: Downey argues power has swung back to founders, who can preempt rounds and choose non-traditional capital because they control their destiny after raising only $4-6M, with some still taking $75M rounds at ~$1.1B valuations.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Founder optionality compresses VC leverage; expect more preemptive offers and founder-favorable structures in hot deals.

insights.claim: Founders increasingly raise late-stage capital not to hire hundreds of people but to fund token spend, a fundamental shift in use-of-funds from headcount/offices to compute.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Late-stage rounds are now compute-financing vehicles; underwrite gross-margin sensitivity to token costs, not burn from headcount.
insights.quote: And they're like, Tken spent.

insights.claim: Tunguz says founder control now manifests through board voting constructs (founders holding outsized voting power) rather than shrinking board size, echoing the founder-friendly dynamics of 2021.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Governance risk for late investors rises; voting control concentration signals 2021-like founder leverage returning.

themes.theme: Capital Sources, VC Value & Round Structuring

insights.claim: Tunguz argues 'tourist' late-stage capital is not dead: crossover funds (T. Rowe Price, Fidelity), BlackRock/Blackstone, and sovereigns (Mubadala, G42) are getting smarter and entering earlier, with Cloudflare cited as a case where a disproportionate share of capital came from non-venture sources.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz / Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Diverse non-VC late-stage capital changes the financing choreography and could displace traditional growth VCs in marquee deals.

insights.claim: Tunguz frames crossover investors as essential bridges from a private to a public investor base, noting a company with Fidelity on its cap table for ~24 months is a reliable precursor to an IPO filing.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz / host
insights.so_what: Crossover ownership is a leading indicator of imminent IPOs; track cap-table changes as a signal.
insights.quote: you can always notice when a company is going to go public because they've had Fidelity on their capture table for 24 months and then shockingly enough, they file

insights.claim: The panel predicts greater concentration of founder-VC partnerships (fewer board investors) as companies need less capital, with VC value concentrated in capital-markets events (IPOs, M&A), customer intros, and board construction.
insights.speaker: host / Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Fewer, higher-value VC relationships per company; differentiation shifts to capital-markets expertise over check-writing.

insights.claim: Tunguz says two-tranche / blended-valuation rounds (a la the alleged 'Sequoia scam') are recurring, estimated at ~5% of rounds and 'not a red herring anymore,' echoing 2021 when three financings could occur in a single year.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Blended-price structuring is rising in hot deals; diligence the true entry price and any prior tranche before pricing your round.
insights.quote: I would guess like still 5% of rounds but ... it's not um it's not a red herring anymore

insights.claim: Downey notes blended-cost structuring (a lead absorbing unraised allotment from a prior round to lower its blended entry) is decades-old, not new, and can be founder-friendly when it consolidates a round.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Context for interpreting 'deceptive' blended-price headlines; the practice can be benign or predatory depending on negotiating leverage.

insights.claim: Doherty notes information rights are not uniform across investors, so transparency on round structure is a nuanced, negotiated matter; she sometimes invests pre-lead and prices her own round.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Co-investors should explicitly ask how a round is structured rather than assume disclosure of blended pricing.

insights.claim: The panel observes venture transparency is cyclical and tracks the NASDAQ: founders air spicy VC grievances at market peaks and go quiet (while VCs impose harsh terms like 4x liquidation preferences) at troughs.
insights.speaker: host / Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Founder candor about VC behavior is itself a market-cycle sentiment indicator; peak transparency suggests we're near a top.
insights.quote: venture transparency is the uh it's it follows the NASDAQ pretty closely

insights.claim: OpenAI pitched Y Combinator companies $2M of tokens in exchange for equity via a SAFT-like 'simple agreement for future tokens,' an emerging way labs invest using inference credits instead of cash.
insights.speaker: host / panel
insights.so_what: Labs can acquire equity cheaply with marginal-cost tokens while locking in future high-margin spend; a novel cap-table dynamic to watch.
insights.quote: the open AI pitch to Y Combinator companies offering $2 million worth of tokens in exchange for equity

insights.claim: Some funds now structure deals where roughly half of a stated investment is delivered as compute (GPU-hours) rather than cash, financializing raw compute as a currency for equity.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Compute-for-equity blurs the line between supplier and investor; effective valuations and dilution must account for in-kind compute.
insights.quote: I'm investing $20 million in a company but half of that 10 million is actually in the form of compute

insights.claim: The panel extrapolates a stack where labs trade tokens for equity, infra providers trade GPU-hours for equity, and ultimately power/electricity access could be exchanged for equity if energy becomes the binding bottleneck.
insights.speaker: host / Michael Downey
insights.so_what: The closer to the metal (compute/power) an investor sits, the stronger their equity-acquisition position in an input-constrained world.
insights.quote: eventually if power is the uh bottleneck you know potentially

insights.claim: Doherty notes OpenAI and Anthropic run aggressive partnership programs giving portfolio companies token allowances, a customer-acquisition strategy that pays off as those companies scale token spend later.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Token-grant programs are land-grab CAC for labs; early allowance recipients are sticky future spenders.

themes.theme: Model Routing, Skill Distillation & On-Prem / Open Source Shift

insights.claim: Tunguz reports a hybrid architecture where state-of-the-art models generate reusable markdown 'skills' that can be run on local/cheaper models, cutting his AI token spend by moving from 65% to ~91% local-model inference as he adds skills.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Skill/model distillation lets apps deliver frontier-quality output at commodity inference cost, structurally pressuring frontier-model revenue per task.
insights.quote: I've gone from 65% to say 91% as of yesterday in terms of local model inference

insights.claim: Tunguz predicts the future application architecture is software companies managing a context database, building skills/instructions, and selecting models so customers get state-of-the-art AI without state-of-the-art prices (e.g., running HubSpot via a frontier model is possible but uneconomic).
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Value accrues to the application layer that bundles/diffuses intelligence cheaply, not to whoever pays raw frontier-model prices.
insights.quote: so that you the customer can operate with state-of-the-art AI without state-of-the-art AI prices

insights.claim: OpenRouter's top weekly models are largely cheap open/foreign models (DeepSeek V4 Flash, Xiaomi MiMo v2.5, Tencent Hunyuan3 preview, MiniMax M3), evidence startups already route inference away from frontier models to dodge cost spikes.
insights.speaker: host / Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Startups are insulated from frontier-model price shocks via routing to open-source; enterprise cost complaints may not hit startup margins.

insights.claim: Tunguz says the first adopters of open-source models were companies with small/negative gross margins (e.g., Cursor), and a dynamic US open-source ecosystem (Nvidia committing ~$23B to open models, Google's Nemotron-style efforts) is critical so apps can compete with labs.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Open-source model funding by Nvidia/Google levels the playing field for low-margin app companies against labs; monitor open-model release cadence.
insights.quote: the very first companies to adopt open source models were the ones with business models with small gross margins

insights.claim: Downey argues model choice will be abstracted away for most users, with routers (e.g., OpenRouter) auto-selecting the lowest-cost/best model and the most regionally optimal compute, analogizing it to picking a spark plug.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Routers become the gatekeeper/tour-guide layer; their auto-switching is the moat, making them a strategic chokepoint to own.
insights.quote: It's like what spark plug do I want in my car?

insights.claim: OpenRouter reportedly raised roughly $113M in the past month, underscoring investor appetite for the model-routing abstraction layer.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Large financing validates routing as a venture-scale category and a defensible position in the AI stack.

insights.claim: The shift toward on-prem/edge inference is driven partly by cybersecurity concerns about running data in the cloud, especially in manufacturing where firms are prime cyber-threat targets.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Security, not just cost, is pushing inference on-prem in regulated/industrial verticals; favors edge/local-model vendors.

themes.theme: Fable 5 / Mythos Model & Frontier Model Economics

insights.claim: Anthropic shipped its 'Mythos' model branded Fable 5, costing about twice as much as Opus 4.8, with Tunguz citing a ~10-percentage-point benchmark jump versus the typical 1-2 points per release (every ~41 days) and agentic coding scores rising from ~13.4 to ~29.3.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: A genuine step-function in agentic coding raises what software can build/self-improve, accelerating product velocity and PMF for AI-native startups.
insights.quote: There's a 10 percentage point improvement

insights.claim: Tunguz characterizes Fable 5 as a slower 'central coordinator' that federates work to sub-agents, orchestrates over long periods, and manages memory effectively, and used it to analyze three codebases and optimize performance overnight.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Long-horizon orchestration is the differentiating capability of the new frontier model, enabling overnight autonomous engineering work.
insights.quote: I think about it as a central coordinator where you give it a task and it will federate uh work to different agents and orchestrate them

insights.claim: Fable 5 is expensive but not the most expensive: certain OpenAI 'pro' models reserved for math/science cost 3-4x more per token, though OpenAI argues GPT-5.5 Pro is token-efficient; rough quoted rates were ~$5/$30 per million in/out for GPT-5.5 vs ~$10/$50 for the Anthropic model.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz / host
insights.so_what: Per-token price rankings differ from effective cost once thinking-token efficiency is included; underwrite total task cost, not headline rates.

insights.claim: Doherty reports portfolio companies use a hybrid model strategy: cheaper models for repetitive tasks and expensive models for higher-level reasoning/orchestration, which Tunguz confirms works exceptionally well.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty / Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Tiered model usage is now standard practice for managing AI COGS; a key margin lever for application companies.

insights.claim: Tunguz expects buyers to demand secure, suite-level (not point) software that improves every two to three days, raising the SaaS subscription value proposition of continuous AI-driven improvement.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Continuous-improvement cadence becomes a competitive requirement; static products will lose to those iterating via agentic coding.

themes.theme: Vertical / Hyper-Specialized Applied AI & Where Value Accrues

insights.claim: Doherty advances a 'hyper-specialized AI' thesis: general models need large amounts of real-world data stored in a company or on a factory floor to become great at specific tasks, and a model is not fully trained on delivery until it gets in-situ experience.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Proprietary in-the-field data is the moat for vertical AI; favors startups that capture operational data over generic model access.
insights.quote: to get them really really good at a specific application task they need a lot of data that is stored ... on a factory floor in real life

insights.claim: Doherty argues vertical AI value accrues to new AI-native startups rather than incumbents, because incumbents must retrofit AI into existing software, and the space remains very early in commercialization despite better models since 2021.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Bet on AI-native vertical startups over incumbents for the next wave of applied-AI value capture.

insights.claim: Maniva (founder ex-DeepMind embodied AI) deploys video-to-robotic-action multimodal models on off-the-shelf edge hardware to do single factory-floor tasks (e.g., candy defect detection), freeing workers for higher-level work.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Edge multimodal AI for industrial automation is a concrete, deployed use case; watch embodied/video-action model startups.

insights.claim: Downey says startups that best simplify onboarding/packaging of AI orchestration for non-Silicon-Valley users will win, which is why OpenAI and Anthropic are funding external consulting orgs (Accenture, Blackstone) to handle the 'assembly required' to cross the adoption chasm.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Distribution/packaging simplicity is the bottleneck to mainstream AI adoption; consulting partnerships and easy onboarding are competitive levers.

insights.claim: Downey frames PE/consulting-AI lab partnerships as a temporary bridge: labs are 'on the clock' post-IPO to prove tangible ROI, so they're loading up on deployment capacity to validate adoption before public-market scrutiny.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Post-IPO ROI accountability could compress the window for the current lab-consulting deployment model; watch for adoption metrics.

themes.theme: Seed Pricing, Power Law & Capital-Source Risk

insights.claim: Carta data shows seed valuations going parabolic: the US 95th percentile reached $174M and the 90th percentile $94M, up from roughly $66M and $50M in 2022.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Top-decile seed entry prices have roughly 2-3x'd since 2022, materially raising the bar for fund-returning outcomes.
insights.quote: the 95th percentile for seed rounds in the US ... now have a valuation of 174 million. 90th percentile 94 million

insights.claim: Downey's provocative take: the top 1-5% of seed deals are likely UNDERpriced because exit outcomes keep getting structurally larger, even if the median seed valuation is somewhat overpriced for the current cycle.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Implies paying up for the very best seed deals is rational; the risk is selection error, not headline price.
insights.quote: that top 1% or top 5% is likely underpriced

insights.claim: Doherty invests on a case-by-case valuation basis, noting AI lets companies charge 3-7x prior software pricing (because the software now does the work), implying proportionally larger eventual exits that can justify higher entry prices.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Higher achievable software pricing reframes what a 'fair' seed valuation is for AI-native companies in expanding markets.
insights.quote: can charge 3 to 7x

insights.claim: Downey identifies the bigger structural risk as LP capital rotation: LPs (family offices, high-net-worths) over-allocated to late-stage secondaries over 2-3 years, will earn generational returns, and may keep chasing pre-IPO deals instead of recommitting to 10-year early-stage funds, shrinking the seed/pre-seed capital supply.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Seed funding supply could contract as LPs prefer late-stage secondaries; a bigger market driver than valuation fluctuation.
insights.quote: the source of capital that kind of feeds that seed stage the preede stage ... compositionally may not be there in the same way

insights.claim: Downey reports that at the pre-seed/day-zero level, great emerging managers still get in at low valuations, contrasting with frothy median seed pricing.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Pre-seed remains a relative-value entry point; backing first-check emerging managers is one way to dodge seed inflation.

insights.claim: Tunguz says venture is splitting into index-style and concentrated funds like public markets, with selection and the power law decisive, citing a VanCap study showing rolling 5-year 75th-95th percentile exits rising far faster than inflation or venture inflation.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Higher entry prices demand sharper GP selection; return dispersion across seed managers should widen.

insights.claim: Tunguz flags AI M&A is constrained by national-security concerns and a tiny buyer pool, estimating fewer than ~10 acquirers can afford a ~$50B exit, raising the question of whether secondary markets shift to the next top-20 companies.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Thin large-cap buyer pool and security scrutiny make IPO/secondaries the main liquidity path for top AI companies; M&A optionality is limited.
insights.quote: the total number of buyers who can afford say um $50 billion exit is probably fewer than 10

insights.claim: Tunguz opposes nationalization of major US AI labs (a topic floated by Bernie Sanders, Sam Altman and Donald Trump), warning of regulatory capture, while acknowledging the US government's Intel stake performed well and that lab-government dual-use relationships (a la Bell Labs) are valuable.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Nationalization risk is a tail policy overhang for AI labs; the consensus among these investors is strongly against it.

insights.claim: Saronic's autonomous drone boats (a Corsair) reportedly rescued the crew of a downed Apache helicopter in the Strait of Hormuz, demonstrating real battlefield deployment of maritime defense-tech autonomy.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Live combat use validates the maritime autonomy thesis; defense-tech demand and revenue are immediate and government-funded.

insights.claim: Downey notes defense-tech companies whose initial demand/revenue comes purely from defense can later extend those value propositions to commercial applications, broadening their TAM.
insights.speaker: Michael Downey
insights.so_what: Defense-funded R&D can spawn commercial spin-offs; watch dual-use expansion as an upside lever for defense-tech investments.

insights.claim: Tunguz pitches MotherDuck (commercializing open-source DuckDB) as ideal for the agent era because many tiny analytics databases can be spun up per agent (a million agents each with their own instance) under a central control layer.
insights.speaker: Tomasz Tunguz
insights.so_what: Lightweight per-agent databases are a structural fit for agentic workloads; open-source-commercialization data infra is a venture theme.

insights.claim: Doherty backs Knox Metals, a next-day metal-servicing platform in Detroit supplying the defense/space hardware supply chain, presented as concrete evidence of American re-industrialization actually cutting steel.
insights.speaker: Paige Doherty
insights.so_what: Metal-servicing is a multi-deca-billion-dollar legacy market ripe for a fast, hardware-startup-aligned supplier; a re-industrialization play.
